title: The eXpress Data Path: Fast Programmable Packet Processing in the Operating System Kernel
authors: Toke Høiland-Jørgensen, Jesper Dangaard Brouer, Daniel Borkmann, John Fastabend, Tom Herbert, David Ahern, David Miller
tldr: Introduces XDP — the earliest hook in the Linux kernel receive path. Achieves near-line-rate (14.88 Mpps) packet processing on a 10 GbE NIC using eBPF programs, enabling DDoS mitigation at microsecond latency.
key_findings: XDP achieves full line-rate packet drops on commodity 10 GbE NICs
Supports four actions: XDP_DROP, XDP_PASS, XDP_TX, XDP_REDIRECT
Outperforms DPDK for drop-based workloads without needing kernel bypass
Enables safe, verifiable kernel-level packet processing using eBPF
project_relevance: This is the foundation of your DDoS mitigation data plane. Your adaptive rate-limiting enforcer (Phase 4) uses XDP/TC eBPF programs. Read this before touching any eBPF code.
reading_focus: Focus on Section 3 (design), Section 5 (performance evaluation), and the XDP action model. Note the difference between native XDP and generic XDP — this matters for Kind clusters.

title: E-GraphSAGE: A Graph Neural Network Based Intrusion Detection System for IoT
authors: Wai Weng Lo, Siamak Layeghy, Mohanad Sarhan, Marcus Gallagher, Marius Portmann
tldr: Applies GraphSAGE to network flow graphs (nodes = IP hosts, edges = flows) using the CICIDS2018 dataset. Achieves competitive IDS performance. The most direct competitor to your project — you must clearly differentiate from this.
key_findings: GraphSAGE outperforms traditional ML (RF, SVM) on flow-graph classification
Edge features (flow statistics) are critical — not just node features
Inductive learning enables handling of unseen nodes (new IP addresses)
Evaluated on CIC-IDS-2018 dataset across 7 attack categories
project_relevance: Your PRIMARY competitor paper. Key differentiator: E-GraphSAGE uses flat IP-flow graphs; YOUR project uses Kubernetes pod/service topology graphs with eBPF telemetry. Know this paper inside out before your literature review presentation.
reading_focus: Study Section IV (graph construction methodology) and Section V (results). Note their node/edge feature design — adapt it for Kubernetes pod features. Their FPR/FNR metrics will be your comparison baseline.

title: Kairos: Practical Intrusion Detection and Investigation Using Whole-System Provenance
authors: Zijun Cheng et al. (University of Cambridge, tfjmp group)
tldr: Builds a provenance graph from kernel audit events (collected via eBPF), applies a streaming attention-based GNN to detect APT attack subgraphs, and provides forensic reconstruction tools. 100% detection on DARPA TC with 1 FP per 4 days.
key_findings: 100% detection rate on all 6 DARPA Transparent Computing APT attack scenarios
Only 1 false positive alert per 4 days on production workloads
eBPF-based provenance graph construction: ~2% CPU overhead, ~150MB RAM
Streaming GNN inference — detects attacks as they unfold, not offline
project_relevance: The CLOSEST existing work to your project. Uses the exact pipeline: eBPF telemetry → interaction graph → GNN anomaly detection. Study their graph construction methodology carefully — adapt it for Kubernetes pod interaction graphs instead of system call provenance graphs.
reading_focus: Focus on Section 3 (provenance graph model) and Section 5 (GNN architecture). Note how they handle the temporal dimension. Their graph schema (entities = processes/files/sockets, edges = syscall interactions) maps to your schema (entities = pods/services, edges = network flows).

title: LUCID: A Practical, Lightweight Deep Learning Solution for DDoS Attack Detection
authors: Roberto Doriguzzi-Corin, Stuart Millar, Sandra Scott-Hayward, Jesus Martinez-del-Rincon, Domenico Siracusa
venue: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management (TNSM) 2020
tldr: CNN-based DDoS detector operating on time-windowed flow feature matrices. Achieves high F1 (>0.99) on CICDDoS2019 with low computational overhead. Strong baseline you must beat in your evaluation.
key_findings: CNN applied to 2D feature matrix (flows × features) — captures temporal patterns
Designed for online detection with bounded latency
Open-source implementation available on GitHub (lucid_ddos)
Evaluated across 12 DDoS attack types with near-perfect accuracy on volumetric attacks
project_relevance: This is your primary DEEP LEARNING baseline in the evaluation chapter. Your evaluation must include a LUCID comparison. Download their code from GitHub and reproduce their results on your testbed traffic.
reading_focus: Study Section IV (feature extraction) — note which 77 features they use. Then compare with what Hubble flow records provide. Their CNN architecture in Fig.2 is your baseline to beat.

title: Inductive Representation Learning on Large Graphs (GraphSAGE)
authors: William L. Hamilton, Rex Ying, Jure Leskovec
tldr: Introduces GraphSAGE — an inductive GNN framework that learns node embeddings by sampling and aggregating from local neighborhoods, enabling generalization to unseen nodes. Essential because Kubernetes pods appear and disappear constantly.
key_findings: Inductive learning — generates embeddings for unseen nodes at inference time
Three aggregator functions: mean, LSTM, max-pooling
Scales to graphs with billions of nodes via mini-batch neighborhood sampling
Outperforms transductive methods (DeepWalk, node2vec) on dynamic graphs
project_relevance: GraphSAGE is your Stage 2 detection model. Its inductive property is CRITICAL for Kubernetes: new pods are created constantly, and you need embeddings for pods never seen during training. Read Sections 2 and 3 carefully.
reading_focus: Pay special attention to Section 2.1 (the aggregation algorithm) — you'll implement this via PyTorch Geometric's SAGEConv layer. Section 3 (theoretical analysis) explains why inductive learning works.

title: Variational Graph Auto-Encoders
authors: Thomas N. Kipf, Max Welling
venue: arXiv / NIPS Workshop on Bayesian Deep Learning 2016
tldr: Introduces Graph Autoencoder (GAE) and Variational Graph Autoencoder (VGAE) — an encoder-decoder framework that learns latent node representations and reconstructs the adjacency matrix. High reconstruction error = anomaly.
key_findings: GCN encoder produces latent embeddings; inner product decoder reconstructs adjacency
VGAE adds a variational (Bayesian) inference layer for better regularization
Reconstruction error is a natural anomaly score — no labels needed
Works on graphs with node features AND purely structural graphs
project_relevance: This is your Stage 1 anomaly detector (unsupervised baseline). Train GAE on NORMAL Kubernetes traffic only — then flag high reconstruction-error graphs as attacks. Only 4 pages — read it today.
reading_focus: The entire paper is short (4 pages). Focus on Equation 1 (ELBO objective), Figure 1 (architecture), and Table 1 (link prediction results). The anomaly detection application is not in this paper — that's YOUR novel application.

title: Semi-Supervised Classification with Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN)
authors: Thomas N. Kipf, Max Welling
tldr: Introduces Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) — spectral-based convolution on graphs using a simplified first-order approximation of Chebyshev polynomials. The foundational paper for most modern GNN architectures.
key_findings: Layer-wise propagation rule: H = σ(D̃^{-1/2} Ã D̃^{-1/2} H W)
Captures information from k-hop neighborhoods with k GCN layers
Semi-supervised: needs only a fraction of labeled nodes
Achieves state-of-the-art on citation network benchmarks
project_relevance: GCN is the mathematical foundation of GraphSAGE and GAE. You MUST understand the propagation rule before implementing your detection model. Also useful if you want to compare GCN vs. GraphSAGE vs. GAE in your ablation study.
reading_focus: Section 2 (spectral graph convolutions) and Section 3 (fast approximate convolutions). The derivation from spectral to spatial convolution explains WHY GCNs work on graphs.

title: Temporal Graph Networks for Deep Learning on Dynamic Graphs
authors: Emanuele Rossi, Ben Chamberlain, Fabrizio Frasca, Davide Eynard, Federico Monti, Michael Bronstein
venue: ICML Workshop on Graph Representation Learning 2020
tldr: Introduces Temporal Graph Networks (TGN) — a general framework for deep learning on dynamic graphs with a memory module per node that captures longitudinal behavior. Processes graph events as a continuous stream rather than snapshots.
key_findings: Per-node memory module updated by interaction events (not snapshot-based)
Combines memory state with neighborhood aggregation at inference time
Generalizes many existing temporal graph methods (JODIE, DyRep, TGAT)
Achieves state-of-the-art on temporal link prediction and node classification
project_relevance: TGN is your optional Stage 3 model for detecting low-and-slow attacks. These attacks evolve over HOURS — TGN's per-node memory captures the longitudinal behavioral shift that snapshot-based GAE/GraphSAGE cannot see. Implement only if time allows.
reading_focus: Section 2 (TGN framework — memory, message, and embedding modules) and Algorithm 1. Then check their official GitHub repo for the PyG-compatible implementation.

title: Kitsune: An Ensemble of Autoencoders for Online Network Intrusion Detection
authors: Yisroel Mirsky, Tomer Doitshman, Yuval Elovici, Asaf Shabtai
tldr: Kitsune is an ensemble of shallow autoencoders trained on feature clusters. Unsupervised — no labels needed. A reconstruction error spike signals anomaly. Strong baseline for unsupervised detection comparison.
key_findings: Feature clustering via correlated feature groups (using PCA correlation)
Each sub-autoencoder trained on one feature cluster
Meta-autoencoder aggregates sub-errors into final anomaly score
Detects novel attacks not seen during training — truly unsupervised
project_relevance: This is your UNSUPERVISED baseline in the evaluation chapter. Compare your GAE against Kitsune's approach. Key question: does graph-structure awareness (GAE) outperform feature-clustering (Kitsune) for Kubernetes DDoS?
reading_focus: Section 3 (Kitsune architecture) and Section 5 (evaluation). Their feature set (115 network statistics) will help you design your own feature extraction. Their GitHub has a usable implementation.

title: XDP in Practice: Integrating XDP into our DDoS Mitigation Pipeline
authors: Gilberto Bertin (Cloudflare)
tldr: Cloudflare's production case study of replacing their DDoS mitigation with XDP. Shows how to separate the data plane (XDP fast path) from the control plane (rule management) using BPF maps. The industry blueprint.
key_findings: XDP matches DPDK performance while maintaining full OS network stack integration
BPF maps enable lock-free rule sharing between XDP programs and userspace
Defense-in-depth: simple volumetric drops in XDP, complex inspection at TC level
Atomic rule updates prevent traffic disruption during mitigation changes
project_relevance: Direct blueprint for your Phase 4 mitigation controller. The separation of control plane (anomaly scores → rules) from data plane (XDP → enforcement) is exactly your architecture. Read before implementing the mitigation controller.
reading_focus: Focus on the architecture diagram (control plane vs. data plane), the BPF map design for blocklists, and the integration with their existing pipeline. This paper is a talk transcript — short and very practical.

title: MicroRank: End-to-End Latency Issue Localization with Extended Spectrum Analysis in Microservice Environments
authors: Guangba Yu et al.
tldr: Models Kubernetes microservice communication as call graphs and applies spectrum-based analysis for root-cause localization of latency issues. Shows that graph modeling of microservices is feasible and informative — for reliability, not security.
key_findings: Service call graph captures causal relationships between microservice latency
Spectrum analysis assigns blame scores to suspect services
Online Boutique benchmark used as evaluation testbed (same app you'll use!)
Graph topology alone contains significant information beyond raw metrics
project_relevance: This paper models Kubernetes service communication as a graph for a different purpose (fault detection). It validates YOUR approach of graph modeling but for security. Study their graph construction — it's very similar to your Phase 2.
reading_focus: Section 3 (call graph construction) and Section 4 (spectrum analysis). Note how they handle the dynamic nature of Kubernetes (pods coming and going). Their Online Boutique testbed setup is directly reusable for your project.

title: Graph Neural Networks: A Review of Methods and Applications
authors: Jie Zhou et al.
tldr: Comprehensive survey of GNN methods (spectral, spatial, attention, recurrent, graph autoencoders) and applications across multiple domains. Essential reading for understanding the GNN landscape before choosing your model.
key_findings: Categorizes GNNs into: convolutional, recurrent, graph autoencoders, and spatial-temporal
Reviews 200+ GNN papers and their applications
Discusses open challenges: scalability, dynamic graphs, over-smoothing
Applications in social networks, biology, chemistry, and NLP
project_relevance: Use this as your GNN background reading for Chapter 2 (Background). It gives you the vocabulary to describe GAE, GraphSAGE, and TGN in your thesis. Also helps identify which GNN variant is most appropriate for dynamic graphs.
reading_focus: Section 2 (taxonomy of GNNs) and Section 3 (applications). You don't need to read all 200+ papers — use this as a map to understand which section of the GNN literature your work sits in.

title: Deep Anomaly Detection on Attributed Networks
authors: Kaize Ding, Jundong Li, Rohit Bhanushali, Huan Liu
venue: SIAM International Conference on Data Mining (SDM) 2019
tldr: Proposes a deep autoencoder framework for node anomaly detection on attributed networks that combines attribute reconstruction error AND structural reconstruction error. Both node features AND graph structure matter for anomaly detection.
key_findings: Anomalies exhibit both attribute anomalies AND structural anomalies
Joint optimization of feature reconstruction + adjacency reconstruction
Weighted combination: score(v) = α·||x_v - x̂_v|| + (1-α)·||A_v - Â_v||
Outperforms methods that only use one of these two signals
project_relevance: This paper directly informs your anomaly score formula (Equation in Chapter 4). The weighted combination of feature and structural reconstruction error is exactly what you proposed. Cite this to justify your anomaly scoring approach.
reading_focus: Section 3 (model formulation) and Equation 3 (joint loss). The α hyperparameter they discuss will be a key tuning parameter in YOUR implementation — note their ablation study results.

title: Representation Learning for Dynamic Graphs: A Survey
authors: Seyed Mehran Kazemi et al.
tldr: Survey of methods for learning on dynamic (time-evolving) graphs. Categorizes approaches by how they handle time: snapshot-based vs. event-driven. Essential for choosing between GAE snapshots and TGN event streams.
key_findings: Two paradigms: discrete-time (snapshot) vs. continuous-time (event-driven)
Snapshot-based: easier to implement, loses fine-grained temporal info
Event-driven (TGN, TGAT, JODIE): richer temporal modeling but complex
Trade-off between temporal resolution and computational cost
project_relevance: Informs your decision on whether to use sliding-window snapshots (your current design) or event-driven temporal graphs (TGN). Also provides the vocabulary for your Background chapter's dynamic graph section.
reading_focus: Section 3 (discrete-time methods) and Section 4 (continuous-time methods) — this is the key comparison. Section 5 (open challenges) provides material for your Discussion chapter.

title: Euler: Detecting Network Lateral Movement via Scalable Temporal Link Prediction
authors: Ryan King et al.
tldr: Applies dynamic GNNs to authentication event graphs (nodes = hosts, edges = auth events) for detecting lateral movement in enterprise networks. Shares your temporal graph intuition but targets a different threat model.
key_findings: Temporal graph model captures how communication patterns evolve over days
Scalable to enterprise-scale graphs with millions of edges
Detects subtle lateral movement that static graph methods miss
Low false positive rate (<0.01%) on production enterprise authentication logs
project_relevance: Euler validates the temporal graph approach for security detection. Cite it in your Related Work to show temporal graph methods have been validated for security — then distinguish by: your focus is Kubernetes + DDoS, not enterprise + lateral movement.
reading_focus: Section 3 (temporal graph model) and Section 5 (comparison with static graph baselines). Note their metric choices — you can adopt the same evaluation framework.

title: Cilium: BPF & XDP for Containers
authors: Thomas Graf, Daniel Borkmann (Isovalent / CNCF)
tldr: Cilium uses eBPF to implement Kubernetes networking, security, and observability entirely in the Linux kernel. Hubble provides a streaming gRPC API for real-time flow records — the data source for your graph construction engine.
key_findings: 3–10× lower latency than iptables-based kube-proxy at 10K+ services
Identity-based security (numeric pod identities, not IP-based) survives pod restarts
Hubble exports structured flow records: source pod, destination pod, protocol, bytes, latency, verdict
CiliumNetworkPolicy supports L7 (HTTP, gRPC, Kafka) policy enforcement
project_relevance: Cilium + Hubble IS your Phase 1 (eBPF observability). Install Cilium first, get Hubble working, and understand the flow record schema before building your graph construction engine.
reading_focus: Read the Hubble documentation: https://docs.cilium.io/en/stable/observability/hubble/ — specifically the Flow API schema. Then read CiliumNetworkPolicy for your mitigation controller.

title: A Survey of Defense Mechanisms Against Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Flooding Attacks
authors: Saman Taghavi Zargar, James Joshi, David Tipper
tldr: Comprehensive taxonomy and survey of DDoS attack types and defense mechanisms. Covers volumetric, protocol, and application-layer attacks. Essential for building your DDoS taxonomy in the Background chapter.
key_findings: Four-layer taxonomy: attack source, attack type, defense mechanism, deployment location
Rate limiting, traffic scrubbing, anycast, pushback, and D-WARD surveyed
Low-and-slow attacks identified as hardest to defend (statistical similarity to normal traffic)
Defense-in-depth recommended — no single mechanism covers all attack types
project_relevance: Use this for your DDoS taxonomy table in Chapter 2 (Background). It also justifies why your multi-method approach (eBPF + graph ML + adaptive mitigation) is needed — no single defense is sufficient.
reading_focus: Table II (taxonomy of DDoS attacks) and Section IV (defense mechanisms). Focus on the limitations column — each limitation you identify corresponds to a gap your project addresses.

title: Inductive Representation Learning on Temporal Graphs (TGAT)
authors: Da Xu, Chuanwei Ruan, Evren Korpeoglu, Sushant Kumar, Kannan Achan
tldr: TGAT introduces time-encoding functions to handle continuous-time dynamic graphs using self-attention. Each interaction is processed with temporal context, enabling detection of attacks whose signatures emerge over extended time windows.
key_findings: Functional time encoding maps timestamps to vector representations
Temporal self-attention aggregates time-aware neighbor embeddings
Inductive — handles unseen nodes and new edges at inference time
Outperforms static GNNs on temporal link prediction tasks
project_relevance: TGAT is an alternative to TGN for temporal graph modeling in your Stage 3. Compare TGAT vs. TGN in your related work — TGAT is attention-based (interpretable) while TGN is memory-based (more powerful). Choose based on your interpretability needs.
reading_focus: Section 3 (TGAT model) and Figure 2 (temporal attention mechanism). The time encoding function (Section 3.2) is novel — understand it before comparing with TGN's memory module.

title: SoK: Challenges and Paths Toward Memory Safety for eBPF
authors: Zhenpeng Lin, Yuhang Wu, Xinyu Xing
tldr: Systematic analysis of memory safety vulnerabilities in the eBPF ecosystem. Catalogues CVEs, identifies 4 vulnerability classes (verifier, JIT, helpers, maps), and evaluates mitigations. Critical for your eBPF threat model.
key_findings: ~1.62–3.74% of memory operations in analyzed eBPF programs are not provably safe
Verifier soundness bugs account for 42% of eBPF CVEs
JIT compiler bugs are architecture-specific (x86, ARM, RISC-V)
Map type confusion enables privilege escalation across security boundaries
project_relevance: Use in your Discussion chapter (Threats to Validity / Limitations section). Your defense system runs eBPF code — acknowledge that eBPF itself has security risks. Also informs your threat model: attackers can use eBPF rootkits to defeat your detection.
reading_focus: Section 2 (vulnerability taxonomy) and Section 5 (mitigation analysis). The threat model section maps directly to your thesis's threat model section.

title: Neural Message Passing for Quantum Chemistry
authors: Justin Gilmer, Samuel S. Schütt et al.
tldr: Introduces the Message Passing Neural Network (MPNN) framework that unifies GCN, GraphSAGE, and most other spatial GNNs under a single formalism. Essential for understanding how all GNN variants relate to each other.
key_findings: Unified framework: message function M, update function U, readout function R
Shows GCN, GGNN, Weave, and others are special cases of MPNN
Edge features are first-class citizens in the MPNN framework
k-step message passing = k-hop neighborhood aggregation
project_relevance: Use MPNN formalism in your Background chapter to describe GAE and GraphSAGE under a unified framework. This is the standard way GNNs are presented in academic papers. Edge features (flow metrics) are important in your graph — MPNN handles this natively.
reading_focus: Section 2 (MPNN framework — just 1 page). Then check Table 1 which shows how existing GNNs map to MPNN. Use Equation (1)–(3) in your thesis Background chapter.

title: BPF Performance Tools: Linux System and Application Observability
authors: Brendan Gregg
tldr: The definitive eBPF reference book by Brendan Gregg. Covers BPF architecture, program types, maps, helpers, and 150+ ready-to-use observability tools. The go-to practical reference for writing eBPF programs.
key_findings: Full treatment of BPF program types: XDP, TC, kprobe, tracepoint, uprobe, socket filter
BPF map types and their performance characteristics
150+ ready-to-use BPF tools for CPU, memory, disk, network, and application tracing
Chapters 10–11 focus specifically on networking and socket eBPF
project_relevance: Your implementation reference bible. Use Chapters 10–11 for network eBPF, Chapter 2 for BPF architecture understanding. When you write your eBPF data collection programs, this is the book to have open.
reading_focus: Chapter 10 (Networking) and Chapter 2 (Technology Background). You don't need to read all 880 pages — use it as a reference. Available as PDF/ebook — get it before starting implementation.

title: Tetragon: eBPF-Based Security Observability and Runtime Enforcement for Kubernetes
authors: Cilium Project (Isovalent / CNCF)
tldr: Kubernetes-native eBPF tool that both observes AND synchronously enforces security policies. First tool to block malicious syscalls before completion (eliminating TOCTOU). Policies defined as Kubernetes CRDs.
key_findings: Synchronous in-kernel enforcement via BPF-LSM — no TOCTOU window
CPU overhead: 2–4% in monitoring mode, 4–8% with active enforcement
Blocked CVE-2024-21626 (container escape) before patch was available
Correlates kernel events with Kubernetes pod/namespace metadata automatically
project_relevance: Tetragon is the ideal enforcement backend for your system. When your graph anomaly detector identifies a DDoS source pod, Tetragon's TracingPolicy CRD can quarantine it in real time. Explore this integration for Phase 4.
reading_focus: Tetragon documentation: https://tetragon.io/docs/ — focus on TracingPolicy and enforcement. The GitHub repo has example policies that can be adapted for DDoS source isolation.

title: Jitterbug: Specification and Verification in the Field — Applying Formal Methods to BPF JIT Compilers
authors: Luke Nelson, James Bornholt, Ronghui Gu, Andrew Baumann, Emina Torlak, Xi Wang
tldr: Formally verified BPF JIT compilers using the Rosette solver-aided language. Found 16 previously unknown bugs across 5 Linux JIT implementations. Establishes the correctness guarantees of the eBPF runtime your project runs on.
key_findings: 16 previously unknown JIT bugs found and fixed — all upstreamed to Linux kernel
Correct-by-construction RISC-V 32-bit JIT verified using Jitterbug
Verifier proves safety; JIT proves semantics are preserved in compilation
SMT-based verification scales to complete JIT implementations
project_relevance: Background reading for your eBPF architecture section. Good for demonstrating depth of knowledge in your Background chapter — eBPF is not just a black box, it has formal verification foundations. Low priority unless you're writing about eBPF security.
reading_focus: Section 1 (Introduction) and Section 2 (Background on BPF JIT). The formal verification details are interesting but not essential for your project. Read to understand trust guarantees.

title: Detecting Distributed Denial of Service Attacks Using Data Mining Techniques
authors: Chia-Mei Chen, Ya-Lin Chen, Hong-Cheng Chang
venue: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing 2022
tldr: Recent survey and evaluation of ML-based DDoS detection in cloud environments. Evaluates classical ML and DL on modern datasets with a focus on Kubernetes deployments — directly comparable evaluation context.
key_findings: XGBoost and Random Forest dominate classical ML approaches (F1 >0.97 on volumetric)
Performance drops to 0.72 F1 on low-and-slow attacks for all tested methods
Kubernetes-specific traffic has different statistical properties than traditional datasets
Feature drift between training and deployment degrades model performance over time
project_relevance: Validates your research motivation: existing ML methods struggle with low-and-slow attacks. Cite this to show the gap your graph-based approach fills. The Kubernetes-specific evaluation context is directly relevant.
reading_focus: Section 4 (evaluation on Kubernetes traffic) and Table 3 (F1 scores by attack type). Note the low-and-slow F1 scores — these are the numbers your system needs to beat.

title: Microscope: Pinpoint Performance Issues with Causal Graphs in Micro-service Environments
authors: Jiaming Lin, Pengfei Chen, Zibin Zheng
tldr: Builds causal graphs from microservice communication logs to pinpoint performance bottlenecks. Demonstrates graph modeling of Kubernetes microservices for non-security purposes — validates the approach for your project.
key_findings: Causal graph construction from microservice call logs with latency annotations
Graph traversal identifies the causal chain of performance degradation
Online Boutique used as evaluation testbed
Graph topology encodes rich information about service dependencies
project_relevance: This paper proves that Kubernetes microservice communication CAN be modeled as a graph productively. Cite it to show graph modeling of microservices is an established approach — you're applying it to security instead of reliability.
reading_focus: Section 3 (graph construction) — note how they build the causal graph from microservice logs. Compare with how you'll build your communication graph from Hubble flow records.

title: Network Intrusion Detection with Graph Neural Network
authors: Multiple groups (CIKM / IEEE workshop papers 2021–2023)
tldr: A growing body of work applying GNNs to network intrusion detection. Search arXiv and IEEE Xplore for the latest papers — this is an active research area with new publications every month.
key_findings: GNN-based IDS consistently outperforms traditional ML on graph-structured network data
Edge feature incorporation improves detection accuracy significantly
Temporal graph methods detect slow attacks that snapshot methods miss
Most work uses CIC-IDS datasets — not Kubernetes-specific (your opportunity)
project_relevance: Search IEEE Xplore for papers from 2022–2025 on this topic. Each new paper is either a baseline to compare against or validation of your approach. Keep your literature review updated.
reading_focus: Search query: `graph neural network intrusion detection Kubernetes`. Filter to 2022–2025. Download 3–5 new papers and add them to your references.bib and Chapter 3.

title: CNCF Cloud Native Security Whitepaper v2
authors: CNCF TAG Security
tldr: Authoritative reference on cloud-native security threats and best practices from the CNCF Security TAG. Covers container/Kubernetes-specific threat models, supply chain security, and runtime security. Excellent source for your threat model section.
key_findings: Four lifecycle layers: Develop, Distribute, Deploy, Runtime — each with distinct threats
DDoS and resource exhaustion listed as Runtime layer threats
Network policy enforcement, observability, and runtime security are key mitigations
Zero-trust networking (identity-based, not IP-based) recommended for Kubernetes
project_relevance: Use for your threat model in Chapter 1/4. The Runtime layer threat model is exactly the context your project operates in. Cite this when justifying why existing edge-based defenses are insufficient for Kubernetes.
reading_focus: Section 3 (Threat Models) and Section 4 (Security Principles). The DDoS-relevant threats are in Section 3.3 (Runtime Security). Short document — read in full.

title: FlowSentryX: XDP/eBPF-Based DDoS Mitigation Framework
authors: FlowSentryX Contributors
tldr: Open-source XDP/eBPF-based DDoS mitigation framework you have already studied. Implements per-source-IP rate limiting and SYN cookie validation at the XDP layer. Your project builds significantly beyond it.
key_findings: Per-source-IP rate limiting using BPF LRU hash maps
SYN cookie validation to defend against SYN flood attacks
XDP-layer packet inspection: no kernel stack overhead
Operates at IP level — no Kubernetes pod awareness
project_relevance: You already know this system. Study the SOURCE CODE carefully — specifically the BPF map design for rate limiting and the XDP program structure. Your adaptive rate limiter in Phase 4 extends this with anomaly-score-driven thresholds.
reading_focus: Clone the repo and read the main XDP C program. Note: (1) how BPF LRU maps track per-IP state, (2) how rate limits are enforced, (3) where you would add pod identity lookup to make it Kubernetes-aware.

title: How to Write a Good Systems Paper
authors: Roy Levin, David Redell (SOSP Program Committee)
tldr: The classic guide to writing systems research papers. Covers what makes a good systems paper: crisp problem statement, clean evaluation, honest limitations, and a clear contribution. Essential reading before writing your thesis chapters.
key_findings: A paper must answer: what is the problem, why does it matter, and what is novel?
Evaluation must be reproducible, fair, and compare against the right baselines
Limitations must be honestly stated — hiding them weakens credibility
The contribution must be stated explicitly: do not make readers guess
project_relevance: Read this before writing Chapter 6 (Evaluation). The evaluation design guidance is particularly relevant. Also use when structuring your weekly presentations — each should answer: problem, approach, novelty, and evidence.
reading_focus: Short article — read it in full. Apply the checklist to your own paper draft. Also read Simon Peyton Jones's 'How to Write a Great Research Paper' (YouTube/slides) for complementary advice.

title: Slowloris: Slow HTTP Denial of Service Attack Tool
authors: RSnake (Robert Hansen)
tldr: Original description of the Slowloris attack — sends partial HTTP headers extremely slowly to hold server connections open indefinitely. Exhausts the connection pool without high traffic volume. Cannot be detected by volumetric methods.
key_findings: Slowloris holds connections open by sending incomplete HTTP headers with long pauses
Requires very little bandwidth — one laptop can take down a non-protected server
Statistical traffic features look normal — rate is low, packets are small
Server-side impact: connection pool exhaustion, not bandwidth exhaustion
project_relevance: Slowloris is your PRIMARY test for whether graph-based detection beats flat-feature ML. Your hypothesis: the GRAPH STRUCTURE (unusual long-lived connections from specific pods, anomalous edge persistence) reveals Slowloris when per-flow statistics cannot.
reading_focus: Short original blog post — read it to understand the attack mechanically. Then install SlowHTTPTest (implements Slowloris) and run it against your testbed to generate training data.
